speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me something that's been very important to you about being in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The most important thing for me being in Australia was my freedom. When I came to Australia, and I said I didn't like it, I didn't like the climate and that I felt lonely. But I didn't tell you how wonderful the Australian people were. When I came from Europe, where there was such a lot of anti-Semitism and horrible murders done to us, and here I came to Australia, and the Australians were wonderful people. They were kind. We had nice neighbors. And all of a sudden, we're free.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what it means to be free? Australia has given us our freedom. And forever I'll be grateful to Australia. Also, I was very happy that I had my children. They didn't come easy to me, because of my sicknesses and all. And my husband had milk bars. And he was never home, and I brought them up myself. And they were always struggling. You know? All those things that migrants go through.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it's a wonderful country to be in. And we were happy to come to Australia because it was very, very, very far from Europe. And we wanted to go as far from Europe as possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever miss any aspects of European life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Any what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Aspects of European life. Did you ever feel it was too strange here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I love the European life, and to this very day. You know? But Australia is very cosmopolitan now and quite European. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: But in those days, when I came, it was very provincial. You know? And the people were just very funny-- very funny. And there was no coffee lunches, you know, like we know today. And the people lived in their houses, you know, not outside. And I am used to-- we were used to in Europe to live outside. People used to meet outside. And this kind of thing. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: My greatest dream was to live in a flat in St. Kilda. That was my greatest dream.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived first in Bentley, then in Carnegie. We bought a house together with my mother and stepfather. That didn't work out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So later on, we got a flat in St. Kilda, where Michael was born. And then we had a milk bar in Acland Street, St. Kilda. And then we got a flat from the agent, who liked the way I looked after the other flat. And he gave us a flat.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Leslie was born in the flat in Blessington, near the beach. And then I realized that living in flat is not such a great thing altogether. You know, I really got accustomed to this life here. And I don't think that any other life I would like anymore. I just think it's wonderful to live in a house. And I have the freedom, and the freedom is first of all. And I have all this space, because Australia is so spacious. It's so beautiful. It's so green.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are your two boys Australian then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, both my children born in Australia. Yes. And you mustn't forget that I've always missed family. You see, we haven't got any extended family. And that is very, very sad. And I miss it terribly when holidays come, and you are supposed to celebrate the holidays with your family. We have such a little table-- there's not-- practically, because my youngest son is still unmarried. There are so few of us. And my husband is a survivor, and none of his family has survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from the very minute, you know, when I came to Australia, we started being Jews again and having-- you know-- Pesach and Rosh Hashanah, I'm always sad. All my holidays were always sad. I can't wait for them to finish, because I get very depressed. And when my youngest son was little, I made friends with a very nice woman, who had a daughter six months younger. But they were in Russia during the war, so she had uncles and aunties. So one day, Michael said-- he was, I think, four or five-- he said to me, how come that Helen got uncle and aunties, and I haven't got any uncle and aunties? You know, that upset me very much. Mind you, my friend said straightaway to him, I'm your auntie. I'm your Auntie [NON-ENGLISH]. To this day, he calls her Auntie [NON-ENGLISH]. You know? But that's what I miss terribly. How would you describe the Jewish upbringing you gave your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I gave them a very intense upbringing, because I am a-- I was a very-- I could see now-- a very intensive mother. And that is due to the Holocaust. I'm a terribly worried mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: You see, I was always telling them about Jewishness and Jewishness and Jewishness. You know, to me, Jewishness became a very, very important thing. I come from a home that was relaxed-- you know?-- even though I went to Jewish school. But because of what I went through, I became extremely Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was always worried about them whenever they went away, a terribly worried mother-- you know-- which is bad for your children. Which I realize now that I only was like that-- I wouldn't have been like that had I not gone through what I did, because I was always petrified of being separated from family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk to people about your experiences during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did I talk to my children? Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk to people, other people, and your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't tell other people til I gave my testimony in the Holocaust Center, until I let myself be interviewed in Holocaust Center. I didn't tell other people. In fact, we exchanged our experiences, because if I talk to survivors, we always finish-- no matter what you are talking about, the end of the topic-- the topic came up of the Holocaust. And even I've got a friend who was born here, and she was never in camp, but with her, because she married a survivor, and she's, you know, does a lot of things for this community, we finish sometimes quite a lot talking about Holocaust. She was an interviewer at the Holocaust Center. She's interviewed a lot of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find out about all of the rest of your relatives?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when my cousin was in Lodz, they had lists of people who have survived. They had a committee. They made a committee in every town in Poland. They made a committee, and in that committee, they had lists of people who have survived. If somebody survived, he came and signed the [INAUDIBLE]. And my cousin started looking for my father, for anybody from the Hasman family. But there was never anybody has been signed there. So we presume that they are dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: And for many, many years, I was going with the idea that my father must be alive somewhere. Maybe he's alive, and he couldn't find us, and we didn't find him. I had such fantasies about it all the time. You know? But now I realize that even if he did survive, he would be a very old man. He would be gone anyhow. But your mother survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother survived with me, and my mother passed away six years ago. And I don't know. I suppose she was looking for him. Yes, she also looked for him through Red Cross.

speaker: interviewer
text: In conclusion, Lusia, why did you want to tell your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you want to tell your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why I went to tell my story. I want to tell my story for quite a few reasons. Well, first of all, I want to tell it because there are already certain people that said that Holocaust has never happened. And I'm a witness that it has happened. People are saying that the crematoriums were built after the war. But I have seen them there, during the war, when I was there. And I have seen what happens to Jewish people, how they take little babies and throw to the ceiling, and their brains splash. I have seen that too. And that's the reason why I want to tell the world what the people did to us, what those Germans did to us. My second reason is I'm doing it for my father. I'm doing it for my brother. I'm doing it for my family, who has perished. I'm doing it in their memories, in their honor. And the third reason is that I want my children and grandchildren to have something after I have gone to know what happened to me, because this is, after all, a part of their life too. Whether they like it or not, they have been born in Australia. They are free people. But my genes-- they have inherited my genes, and they are not so good. I'm a nervous woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there last thing you want to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Say it louder.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any last thing you want to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: The last thing-- the last thing I want to tell is that I hope that never ever there will be another Holocaust, no matter for who, no matter for what nation, no matter for what religion, no matter for what race. I want to say that it's about time people stop killing each other. There's no point in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Lusia. Thank you. What side are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do I do with them when they come?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Am I allowed to point? This is my brother. And his name was Rysio. That's my brother in the group of children. And this is the only photograph that I have of my relatives, my family. The only one, and it was found by accident. My cousin, who was also from Lodz, she was liberated in Czechoslovakia. And she was coming back home. And she went to our old apartment, and she found it on the floor. And I brought-- and she gave it to my mother, which she then gave it to me. And I made all different kind of enlargement out of that. He was a very, very little boy. And we were on a vacation. And he was five years younger than me. And most of the people-- this little girl on the left side, I knew her parents too. She had a sister my age. And the parents-- her parents and my parents were good friends. And they always went together on holidays. And all those parents got together and got this governess for a few hours a day, so they can have a bit of rest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was made in a very nice place called Kreshev. There was a guest house there. And as you can see, it had beautiful, beautiful forests. The forests went for miles and miles and miles. Really beautiful. Always the trees with needles-- what do you call them? Pine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pines. Pines.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what date this photograph was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think, I think, to the best of my knowledge, I think it was taken the summer before the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who is this, Lusia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as you have seen before, the picture of the group of children, this is an enlargement of my brother. You know how little he was there. This is my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he was such a clever boy. His name was Rysio Hasman. And I must tell you something. He was such a clever boy. I'm not joking. At the age of six or seven, he would have grown up to be a mathematical genius. I'm not exaggerating. He was so brilliant in mathematics, as little, as young as he was. And a very funny thing happened to him. When he was about five or six years old, he woke up one morning with a silver patch of hair, gray patch of hair out of nothing. He was such a clever boy. And he loved me so much. And I miss him so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Lusia. Can you tell me about this photograph, Lusia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is the house where we used to live in Lodz. Of course, it looks different now, because-- you see the shop on the left side that's got the awnings? It didn't look like that. That was our shop. Now, they've got their grocery. And that was our shop. And it looked altogether different.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived behind the shop. How it links-- uh, I don't know how to explain it how it links to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Through the door.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the stairs, yes. It was quite a big apartment. And some people-- see, I've never been back to Poland. But some people that I know and were from Lodz, when they told me they're going back to Poland to Lodz, I said, please, please bring me a picture of the house I used to live in. And it's 121 [NON-ENGLISH] Street. It was really beautiful before the war, but now it looks very, very dilapidated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell me about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is a picture of our Kibbutz, that I mentioned to you, in Gailingen. And all these young people are my friends. And they all went to Israel. I am the first girl with folded hands in the front. Can you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me. And the girl that's standing out there, is in the white, this is a friend of mine in Israel. And the girl that is next to the boy is also a friend of mine. She was our [NON-ENGLISH]. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The photograph was taken in Gailingen, in 1946. There were more people in our Kibbutz. But as it happened, these are the ones that were in the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, that's yours truly. [LAUGHS] It's my passport picture when I came to Australia in 1950. That was my passport picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't believe it's me. Well, this picture that you see here, it's my mother and my stepfather. It's been made in Konstanz, in Germany before we came to Australia. Well, look how young my mother is here. That's the man she married. As I said before, her name was Adela. But after we came to Australia, she started to call herself Ada. And that's my stepfather. And his name was Abram Eichenbaum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I've just forgotten to mention before, when you were showing my stepfather, that his name was Abram Eichenbaum. And I forgot to mention he's just passed away not long ago. And I feel I'd like to say that he himself was a survivor. And he lost his first wife and two children in the Holocaust. And I feel very sorry that he went. I want you to-- I wanted really to mention that he is a survivor and lost all his family in the Holocaust. Now, this here is my wedding picture. It was taken 1949, in Konstanz, when I married my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: These here are my two boys when they were little. The older one is Michael. And the little one in the pram is Leslie. Of course, now they are now quite grown up. But that's what they looked like when they were young boys. Weren't they gorgeous?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this here is Michael, my oldest son. And this is his bar mitzvah picture, so he was 13 here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's about the only picture I have from his bar mitzvah. This is my younger son Leslie. He calls himself Les now. I don't really remember how old he was at the time the picture was taken, but he looks a little bit different today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my younger son Les. And unfortunately, he cannot be with us here today, because he went for six month to New York. He's working there. And I'm very, very upset about it. I wish you were here, Les. But nevertheless, here you are and it is like you are with us. And you look different than what you looked in your last picture. And we miss you. I wish you were here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Lusia, would you like to introduce your family to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, with pleasure. That's my husband Harry. That's my older son Michael. That's his wife, Sylvia. That's his son Daniel. That's Helen. She's mom of my granddaughter Isobel. And unfortunately, my younger son is not here with us to be in the group, but you have seen the picture. You will see his picture on the video.

speaker: interviewer
text: Harry, would you like to say a few words?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, I will say just a few words. Look, after everything what's happened to me and to my wife, still we have managed to be a good couple. We have really a nice life, you know. And we-- we try not to remember what was happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And Michael? [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I'll just say a couple of words.

speaker: interviewer
text: Michael, would you like to add your comments?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a couple of words, I guess. Mom and Dad tried to provide for my brother Les, who's currently in New York on a study trip, and myself with as normal a background, as normal a family upbringing as possible. Mostly, we didn't really feel any different from anybody--

speaker: interviewer
text: Michael, would you like to add your comments?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll say a couple of words. Let me explain, first of all, that my brother Leslie, who would normally be here, is in New York. He's there for six months, so he couldn't be with us. And Les, of course, is dad of Isobel. Yeah, on behalf of Les and also myself, I would say just a few words. I'd say that Mom and Dad, despite their experiences, have tried to provide us with a normal family upbringing. And as far as I can remember, by and large, that's what it's been like. We've had very good opportunities, probably better opportunities than many people in Australia ever since Mom and Dad came here. I was born in 1950. And I had very excellent schooling opportunities. And Mom and Dad provided economically as best as they could. They tried very hard. They were very fair parents to both me and Les. They treated us both very equally and very fairly. All that is very, very positive, and it's a credit to them that despite the hardship of their lives, before 1950, which is something that I can't really relate to, because I wasn't there, I wasn't born, it's a credit to them that they could bring up Les and myself as two nearly normal people. [LAUGHTER] No, I think we're probably reasonably normal. At times though, you'd have to say that we probably are sort of a second generation Holocaust pair of brothers. That's a bit hard to explain in 30 seconds, and I won't try. But we certainly grew up against a sort of tough tide of times. And I think a lot of that would have probably come from Mom and Dad's experiences during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: However, what can you say? We made the best of it. And I think at the end of the pipeline, if you like, we both came out pretty well. So you know, it's a pretty good outcome.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I suppose if what we're saying is let's not forget, and let's stick it up the enemy, if you like, I think we've come out of it as more than survivors, that we've come out of it, on behalf of all of us, we've come out pretty good. I'm sure that's true for many other families too. Thanks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would just like to add a few more words before the conclusion. I would say that, looking around me, while we sit here like that, you know, three generations, I feel very victorious. And I think that despite what Hitler wanted to do to us, completely wipe us out from this face of the earth, he did not succeed, because here we are-- us, our children, and our grandchildren. That's all I want to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
